name: Greenwich
title: Where the world sets its clock
note: Pictures for the big-picture opening: the observatory, the Prime Meridian line, and the sea-clock that solved longitude.

items.caption: The Royal Observatory at Greenwich, where astronomers mapped the sky to help sailors find their way, and where the world agreed to start measuring time and place.
items.kidcaption: The Royal Observatory, where scientists studied the stars to help ships cross the oceans without getting lost.

items.caption: The Prime Meridian, zero degrees longitude. Every place on Earth is measured east or west from this exact line, and the world's clock, Greenwich Mean Time, starts here.
items.kidcaption: Stand on this line and you have one foot in the east of the world and one in the west! The whole planet measures distance and time from right here.

items.caption: John Harrison's H1, one of the sea-clocks that finally solved 'longitude', letting sailors know exactly where they were and helping Britain rule the waves.
items.kidcaption: This amazing clock helped solve one of the hardest puzzles ever: how do you know where you are in the middle of the ocean? The answer was a really, really good clock.
